article.title: TypeScript 6.0 Beta Ships as the Last JavaScript-Based Release Before the Go Rewrite
article.summary: Microsoft's TypeScript 6.0 beta marks the end of the original JavaScript codebase, clearing the path for a native Go rewrite in TypeScript 7.0 that promises 10x faster compilation.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Microsoft released the beta of TypeScript 6.0 on February 11, 2026, describing it as a "transition release" and confirming it will be the last major version of the language built on the current JavaScript codebase. The milestone clears the runway for TypeScript 7.0, a full native rewrite in Go internally codenamed "Project Corsa," which has already demonstrated compilation speedups of up to 10x in real-world codebases.

As stated in the [official TypeScript blog announcement](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/typescript/announcing-typescript-6-0-beta/): "We intend for it to be the last release based on the current JavaScript codebase."

## What We Know

### TypeScript 6.0 Is a Bridge, Not a Feature Release

The beta is described as "feature stable," with the TypeScript team signaling no further features or breaking changes before the final release. According to [InfoQ](https://www.infoq.com/news/2026/02/typescript-6-released-beta/), TypeScript 6.0 focuses on eliminating technical debt and standardizing configurations rather than introducing new capabilities. Only patch releases â€” security fixes and high-severity regressions â€” are planned for the 6.x line going forward.

The immediately preceding codebase, internally called "Project Strada," has served as the foundation for every TypeScript release since the language's 2012 debut.

### Sweeping Default Changes

Despite its billing as a minimal feature release, TypeScript 6.0 ships with significant changes to its default configuration. Strict mode, long recommended but historically opt-in, is now enabled by default. The `module` setting defaults to `esnext` instead of `commonjs`, `target` advances to `es2025`, and the `types` field now defaults to an empty array rather than auto-enumerating all `@types` packages â€” a change the TypeScript team flags as likely to require explicit additions such as `"types": ["node"]` in most existing projects.

Other default changes include `noUncheckedSideEffectImports` now enabled, and `rootDir` set to the tsconfig directory by default.

### New Language Features

While modest in scope, TypeScript 6.0 does include several forward-looking additions as detailed in the [official announcement](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/typescript/announcing-typescript-6-0-beta/):

- **ES2025 target and lib support**, including built-in types for `RegExp.escape`, `Promise.try`, and Iterator and Set methods
- **Temporal API types** for the Stage 3 proposal, accessible via `--target esnext` or an explicit `temporal.esnext` lib option
- **Node.js subpath imports** with the `#/` prefix, supported under `node20`, `nodenext`, and `bundler` module resolution
- **`--moduleResolution bundler` combined with `--module commonjs`**, previously an unsupported pairing, now enabled for gradual migrations
- **DOM library consolidation**, folding `dom.iterable` and `dom.asynciterable` into `lib.dom.d.ts` directly
- **`--stableTypeOrdering` flag**, a diagnostic tool to identify type ordering differences between TypeScript 6.0 and 7.0 ahead of the migration

### Deprecations Targeting TypeScript 7.0 Removal

TypeScript 6.0 formally deprecates a long list of legacy options that will be removed in TypeScript 7.0. These include `target: es5`, the `--downlevelIteration` flag, `--moduleResolution node` (the original `node10` resolution), module formats `amd`, `umd`, and `systemjs`, `--baseUrl`, `--moduleResolution classic`, the `--outFile` option, and the legacy `module` keyword used for namespaces. Developers can suppress deprecation warnings during the transition period by adding `"ignoreDeprecations": "6.0"` to their tsconfig.json.

A migration tool, `ts5to6`, is available to help update tsconfig files automatically.

### TypeScript 7.0: The Go Rewrite

The context for TypeScript 6.0's minimal scope is the engineering work underway on TypeScript 7.0. According to a [December 2025 progress update from the TypeScript team](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/typescript/progress-on-typescript-7-december-2025/), the native Go compiler â€” `tsgo` â€” is now feature-rich enough for broad experimentation. The team published benchmark data against real-world codebases:

- **VS Code** (approximately 1.5 million lines of TypeScript): 89 seconds down to 8.74 seconds â€” a 10.2x improvement
- **Sentry**: 133 seconds down to 16 seconds â€” an 8.2x improvement
- **TypeORM**: 15.8 seconds down to 1.06 seconds â€” a 9.9x improvement

The gains stem from Go's native compilation and from multi-threaded type checking, something the single-threaded JavaScript-based compiler never achieved.

Of approximately 20,000 compiler test cases, the Go port produces errors in all but 74 cases where TypeScript 6.0 does, with remaining discrepancies described as intentional changes or work still in progress.

The TypeScript 7.0 native preview is already installable via `npm install -D @typescript/native-preview`, which provides the `tsgo` command. A corresponding VS Code extension updated daily is available on the VS Code Marketplace.

### Why Go?

Microsoft has explained that Go's suitability stems from its functional programming patterns, which align with the shape of the existing TypeScript codebase, while providing automatic garbage collection and native-speed execution. The team has also noted that multi-threaded type checking â€” a key driver of the performance gains â€” was architecturally unavailable in the JavaScript-based compiler given JavaScript's single-threaded event model.

## What We Don't Know

- The exact release dates for TypeScript 6.0 final and TypeScript 7.0 remain unconfirmed. The team has described a "compressed timeline," with 7.0 intended to follow shortly after 6.0, and general availability discussed in community channels for Q2 2026.
- TypeScript 7.0 does not yet implement the full JavaScript emit pipeline; downlevel compilation is currently limited to ES2021, and `--watch` mode behavior may differ from the current compiler in some scenarios.
- The existing TypeScript compiler API ("Project Strada") will not be supported in TypeScript 7.0. A replacement "Corsa" API is under development, but tooling that depends on programmatic access to the compiler â€” including lint plugins, build tools, and code generators â€” will require updates whose coordination has not yet been publicly announced.
- `@enum` and `@constructor` JSDoc tags are not recognized by the new compiler, representing a gap for codebases that rely on these patterns for JavaScript interoperability.

## Analysis

TypeScript 6.0's positioning as a bridge release is practically unusual for a major language version bump. Rather than delivering a feature-heavy release, the team is using the major version boundary to enforce ecosystem cleanup â€” pushing projects off legacy module systems, ES5 targets, and configuration patterns that accumulated over more than a decade.

The deprecations in 6.0 map almost exactly to the features removed in 7.0. This strategy gives the large TypeScript ecosystem time to adapt, while giving the compiler team a clean break point from which to ship a fundamentally different runtime.

The 10x performance benchmarks are striking, but the more significant shift may be architectural. By moving to a multi-threaded model, TypeScript 7.0 can leverage modern multi-core hardware in a way that JavaScript's event loop never permitted. For large monorepos and full-stack TypeScript applications â€” where compilation times measured in minutes have become a genuine productivity obstacle â€” the improvement represents a qualitative change rather than a marginal one.

The ecosystem impact of the API transition remains the most significant open question. Tools that use the TypeScript compiler API for static analysis, code transformation, or IDE integration represent a substantial surface area. Coordinating that upgrade across hundreds of popular packages will take time beyond the compiler's own release.
